No modification to recording. Unfortunately the bird was a bit far away and high up, hence lack of volume. It was on a dry, high snag (I suspect a regular drumming post) in partially cleared, lowland dry dipterocarp forest, with bananas planted, at edge of old-growth.

Same bird as in previous upload. No modification to the recording. This is NORTHERN SOOTY WOODPECKER Mulleripicus funebris, race/subspecies is the nominate funebris. This is now regarded as distinct from SOUTHERN Sooty Woodpecker Mulleripicus fuliginosus, but not yet 'split' on Xeno-Canto. The bird was on a roadside high snag in a semi-urban area adjacent to older growth forest.

Location: Mt. Pulag National Park (near  Kabayan), Benguet, Cordillera Administrative Region
Remarks: The recording was made inside a mossy forest.  I'm not sure if the single sharp calls near the end was the same bird that was singing. I observed further up the trail that those single sharp calls were made by a dark colored bird that can be barely seen in the undergrowth consisting of ferns. Two birds were calling from each side of the trail. One bird quickly climb about 6 inches up a small brush and went down fast. It had a long tail and the movements were quite agitated while emitting the single alarm calls or contact calls, which was being answered at the opposite side of the trail by another bird.
Singing from the top of an exposed reed stem.
